numbers.comment: manual.txt's "Fundamental syntax rules" allows a single quote as a digit separator/padding, same as "_" (e.g. "1'000'000") -- since "'" is otherwise the string-quote character, every numeric alternative below now accepts it too, matching the same fix applied to the standalone tokenizer (tokenizer.ts's NUMBER_PART). The float pattern also now covers the two "f"-suffixed forms the manual documents that don't require a ".": a bare exponent with no decimal point ("5e10") and a plain integer marked as float purely by a trailing "f" ("5f", the *only* way to mark a dot-less, exponent-less literal as floating-point) -- neither matched before, nor did a trailing "d" (an explicit, optional decimal-number suffix, analogous to "h"/"b"/"o"/"q") on an otherwise plain decimal.

label-definition.comment: The "::" (area label) case must come before the plain single-":" cases below: they only look at the first ":", so "area::" would otherwise match as an ordinary label and leave the second ":" unstyled.

purge-statement.comment: The names following "purge" are macro/instruction-class names being un-defined, not keywords — without this taking priority, a purged name that happens to spell a reserved word (e.g. "dword?"/"qword?", real macro names generated by fasmg's own packages/x86/include/macro/proc64.inc's "locals?" macro) would be wrongly highlighted as the unrelated size-specifier keyword.

struct-body.comment: A struct's own field names are plain member identifiers, not keywords — without this as its own block, a field named e.g. "segment" or "offset" (both real field names in fasmg's own packages/x86/projects/challenger/challenger.asm) would be wrongly highlighted as the unrelated "segment" format directive, since the generic #directives pattern otherwise matches that word anywhere.

member-access.comment: "IDENT.field" (e.g. "PLANE_POINTER.offset", struct field access with no space around the dot) must be recognized as a unit before a field name is left to fall through to #directives/#data-directives/#size-specifiers: a field that happens to spell a real directive/register (e.g. "segment", both a real field name in fasmg's own packages/x86/projects/challenger/challenger.asm and the real segment-override directive) would otherwise light up as that unrelated keyword. The lookbehind (only firing right after an identifier/register char, no space) is what tells this apart from a dot-prefixed local label used as a bare operand, e.g. "jmp .exit".

constant-definition.comment: ":=" (defined exactly once, forward-reference-safe) and "=:" (preserves the previous value, restorable with `restore`) must be checked before the plain "=" pattern -- fasmg requires no space between the two characters (confirmed against the real compiler: "X : = 1" fails to assemble, parsed as a label "X:" followed by an invalid "= 1"), so unlike "equ"/"reequ" this isn't a separate keyword the plain "=" pattern could otherwise be taught to skip over.

patterns.comment: "load NAME[:size] from ADDRESS" defines NAME by reading bytes back out of an output area (see fasmg's own packages/x86/include/macro/proc64.inc: "load value:byte from area:pointer").

"define NAME EXPR"/"redefine NAME EXPR" tags NAME as a constant, matching equ/load/:= -- previously NAME fell through unstyled (see fasmg's own packages/x86/include/win32wx.inc: "define _winx.code? _code"). Matching the whole dotted+weak name (including any "?") in one capture also keeps it a single token instead of splitting across #member-access's punctuation/member scopes.

calm-commands.comment: fasmg's CALM (Compiled Assembly-Like Macro) sub-language, used inside calminstruction/postpone bodies — kept as its own scope (matching hover's distinct "CALM command" tag) rather than lumped in with ordinary directives, since it's a genuinely different sublanguage. "call" and "jno" are deliberately left out despite also being CALM commands: both are far more commonly the real x86 CALL/JNO instructions, and this grammar has no notion of "inside a calminstruction body" to disambiguate the two.
calm-commands.name: keyword.other.calm.fasm

procedure-macros.comment: proc/endp/locals/endl/uses/frame/endf/invoke/cinvoke/stdcall/ccall/fastcall/library/import/export/directory/resource/dialog/enddialog/dialogitem/bitmap/icon/cursor/menu/accelerator/versioninfo/resdata are not core fasmg directives — they're ordinary macros from the standard packages/x86/include/macro/proc32.inc, proc64.inc, import32.inc, import64.inc, export.inc, and resource.inc (e.g. "macro invoke?: proc*,args&", "macro library? definitions&", "macro dialog label,title,..." -- dialogitem/enddialog are themselves defined *inside* dialog's own body, purged again at "enddialog"), the same category as any other user-defined macro name, and any x86 program is free to not include these packages at all. But in practice virtually every real fasmg Windows/Linux program uses them (see packages/x86/examples), so leaving the whole family uncolored costs more in everyday readability than the purity of "not a real keyword" is worth. Kept as its own support.* scope rather than folded into #directives (keyword.control.directive.fasm), which is reserved for constructs the core language itself defines.
procedure-macros.name: support.function.fasm

data-directives.comment: Directives that reserve/declare typed storage (db/dw/dd/... and their r-prefixed "reserve" counterparts) get the same storage.type family as #size-specifiers, since they're the same underlying concept (a type/width), just declaring storage instead of just naming a width.
data-directives.name: storage.type.data.fasm

format-directive.comment: format-keywords' own word list (PE, GUI, console, on, at, native, ...) is exactly the kind of short, common word that collides with an ordinary symbol used elsewhere in the file -- e.g. win32wx.inc's own "if ~ definite PE & ~ definite x86.mode" tests whether the symbol PE has been defined, completely unrelated to the format directive of the same spelling. Scoping the word list to only apply within a "format ..." directive's own line (format arguments are always single-line) fixes that false positive the same way #struct-body already protects field names from #directives.

mnemonics.comment: Generated from the full mnemonic list in server/src/data/instructions.json (the same data hover/completion use) rather than hand-picked, after finding the previous hand-picked list covered only 159 of its 1271 real x86 mnemonics -- e.g. lodsb/cmpsb/scasb (calculator.asm's own "lodsb" had no color) and the entire SSE/AVX/legacy-FPU/BMI families were missing. grammar.test.ts's "every instructions.json mnemonic gets keyword.other.mnemonic" test regenerates this same set and fails if the two ever drift apart again.
mnemonics.name: keyword.other.mnemonic.fasm

special-symbols.comment: fasmg's built-in, never-user-defined pseudo-variables: "$"/"$$"/"$@" (current address / current addressing-space base / current reserved-data-block base), "$%"/"$%%" (offset within the output file at which initialized data would land from here / the current actual output-file offset — distinct from the in-memory "$", e.g. fasm2's own source/macos/macho.inc: "$%? = $%?-($-address)"), and "%"/"%%" (current repetition number / total planned repetitions, inside repeat-while-iterate) — kept as their own scope (matching hover's distinct "Built-in symbol" tag) rather than falling through to the generic operator class, since e.g. "if % = %%" (a real, common check inside fasmg's own prologue/epilogue macros) is easy to misread as an operator soup without this. "$%%"/"$%" must be listed before the bare "$" alternative -- alternation here takes the first alternative that matches at all, not the longest, so listing bare "$" first would always win and never let "$%" match. Checked after #numbers, which already claims a "$" immediately followed by a hex digit (fasmg's "$1A"-style hex literal) — a bare "$"/"$$"/"$@" is never one of those. The "%"/"%%" alternatives are guarded against adjacent identifier characters: fasmg does not treat "%" as a special standalone token at all (manual.txt's own special-character list omits it) — it is simply a valid, if unusual, character within an ordinary name, e.g. packages/x86/include/pcount/kernel32.inc's own "BackupRead% =  7" defines a symbol literally named "BackupRead%", whose "%" must not light up as if it were this pseudo-variable.
special-symbols.name: variable.language.special.fasm

operators.comment: "defined"/"definite"/"used"/"eq"/"eqtype" are core fasmg logical/type operators (manual.txt's "Symbols and values" section), the same family as and/or/not -- previously missing entirely, so e.g. win32wx.inc's own "if ~ definite PE & ~ definite x86.mode" left "definite" completely unstyled. "#" (token-pasting -- concatenates the identifiers on either side into one name with no space, e.g. export.inc's own "names.name#%") was likewise completely unstyled before, falling through as plain punctuation. "bappend"/"lengthof"/"elementof"/"scaleof"/"metadataof"/"elementsof"/"trunc" are the same "Expression values" operator family -- added here since they're distinctive enough not to collide with an ordinary identifier; "scale"/"string"/"metadata"/"float" are documented in hover.ts's VALUE_OPERATORS too but deliberately left out of this list, since those four are common enough as ordinary variable/field names (e.g. a UI zoom factor called "scale", or a "float" flag/value) that blanket-highlighting them here would be a real false-positive risk, the same reasoning #calm-commands already applies to "call"/"jno".
operators.name: keyword.operator.fasm
